coredata.dc:title: The effect of surface charge on the cytotoxicity and uptake of carbon quantum dots in human umbilical cord derived mesenchymal stem cells
coredata.dc:creator: Yan, Junyan
Hou, Shulan
Yu, Yanzhen
Qiao, Yong
Xiao, Tongqian
Mei, Yan
Zhang, Zhijun
Wang, Bin
Huang, Chih-Ching
Lin, Chia-Hua
Suo, Guangli
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are emerging as an ideal agent for efficient stem cell labeling. In current study, we synthesized a series of CQDs carrying different surface charges by changing the mass ratio of diammonium citrate (DC) and spermidine (Spd), and evaluated the effects of different surface charges on the cytotoxicity, cellular uptake, stability in human umbilical cord derived mesenchymal stem cells (hUCMSCs). We ascertained the optimal labeling time (24 h) and subtoxic concentration (50 μg/mL) of all different charged CQDs. Our results demonstrated that, although positively charged CQDs are more cytotoxic and have lower photoluminescence (PL) compared to negative CQDs, they still have higher labeling efficiency for their higher uptake capacity. We found that relatively weak positive surface charges enabled CQDs to possess good biocompatibility and labeling efficiency in hUCMSCs. This work will helpfully contribute to the design and optimization of CQDs for tracking stem cells and further benefit to clinical research and application.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Human umbilical cord derived mesenchymal stem cells
Cytotoxicity
Surface charge
Labeling efficiency

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: qRT-PCR analysis of mRNAs expression levels
Effects of CQDs on the osteogenic and adipogenic differentiations of hUCMSCs
Single cell sphere formation in vitro
Synthesis and characterization of CQDs carrying different charges
Cytotoxicity effects of CQDs on hUCMSCs
Effects of surface charges on the uptake of CQDs in hUCMSCs
Effects of surface charges on the stability of CQDs in hUCMSCs
Effect of CQDs on stem cell potentials of hUCMSCs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work has been generously supported by Ministry of Science and Technology (MOST) (Grant 2017YFA0104301 ), National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant 31471307 ), (MOST) (Grant 2014CB965003 ), and One Hundred Person Project from Chinese Academy of Sciences to G.S, as well the scientific research start-up funding of Shaoxing University (Grant No. 20185009 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The characteristics of CQDs synthesized with different mass ratios of diammonium citrate (DC) and spermidine (Spd). (A) Zeta potentials of the four types of CQDs. (B) TEM images of CQDs. Insets in (B): The high resolution TEM images to show lattice structures of corresponding CQDs. (C) UV-Vis absorption spectra of CQDs aqueous solutions at the concentration of 0.1 mg/mL. (D) Fluorescence spectra of CQDs aqueous solutions (0.01 mg/mL) at 365 nm excitation wavelengths. Insets in (C and D): Photographs of the corresponding solutions (C) before and (D) after excitation with a UV lamp (365 nm).

Cytotoxicity effects of CQDs on hUCMSCs. (A) Cell viability of hUCMSCs incubated with CQDs for 72 h. Oxidative stress of 50 μg/mL (B) and 100 μg/mL (C) of CQDs on hUCMSCs after 24 h of incubation. “*”: p < 0.05, “**”: p < 0.01 compared to the control.

Uptake of four types of CQDs in hUCMSCs. (A and B) Uptake kinetic curves of different types of CQDs as function of different incubation times. The cells were treated with 50 μg/mL of CQDs for 0, 3, 6, 9, 12, 24, 48 and 72 h. (C) Uptake kinetic curves of different CQDs as function of different concentrations of CQDs. The hUCMSCs were treated with 50, 100, 200, and 400 μg/mL of CQDs for 24 h. (D) Relative proliferation of hUCMSCs during 24 h of treatment with 50 μg/mL of CQDs. (E, F) The labeling efficiencies of CQDs in hUCSMCs. The hUCMSCs were incubated with CQDs (50 μg/mL) for 24 h and performed flow cytometric analysis. The excitation wavelength was 488 nm, and the FITC fluorescence channel was used to detect cellular content of CQDs. (G) The intracellular distribution of CQDs observed using fluorescent microscope. Scale bar = 100 μm. (H) The retention efficiencies of four types of CQDs in hUCMSCs. (I) The fluorescent density of four types of CQDs in one hUCMSC. For each type of CQDs, thirty cells were used for calculation. Error bars represent standard deviations from three repeated experiments. “**”: p < 0.01 compared to the control (cells without CQDs treatment). “Ctl” represents without CQDs treatment control. Scale bar represents 100 μm in (G).

Stability of CQDs in hUCMSCs. (A) The kinetic curves of fluorescence intensity of CQDs in hUCMSCs as function of culture times after labeling. (B) The kinetic curves of stability of CQDs as function of culture times after labeling. Red color “*”: p < 0.05, C-d compares with C-a and C-b. Yellow color “*”: p < 0.05, C-c compares with C-a and C-b. (C) The proliferation of hUCMSCs labeled with four differently charged CQDs. “*”: p < 0.05 compared to the control. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Effect of CQDs on stem cell potentials of hUCMSCs. (A) Fluorescence images and (C) number count of single cell sphere formation for hUCMSCs treated with four types of the CQDs. The hUCMSCs were suspension cultured for 14 days and live spheres were stained with Calcein-AM. The spheres with diameter ≥50 μm were counted. (B) The representative images of osteogenic and adipogenic induction of hUCMSCs labeled with different of CQDs. (D) Mineralization was quantified through the elution of alizarin red S. (E) The lipid vacuole formation was quantified through the elution of oil-red O. The mRNA levels of Oct-4 (F), Nanog (G) and SOX2 (H) were analyzed by qRT-PCR method. “**”: p < 0.01 compared to the control. Scale bars represent 100 μm in (A and B). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

head.ce:title: The effect of surface charge on the cytotoxicity and uptake of carbon quantum dots in human umbilical cord derived mesenchymal stem cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: CAS Key Laboratory of Nano-Bio Interface, Suzhou Institute of Nano-Tech and Nano-Bionics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Suzhou, Jiangsu, 215123, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: CAS Key Laboratory of Nano-Bio Interface
Suzhou Institute of Nano-Tech and Nano-Bionics

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Bioscience and Biotechnology, National Taiwan Ocean University, Keelung, 20224, Taiwan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biotechnology, National Formosa University, Yunlin, 63208, Taiwan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: e
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center for Clinic Stem Cell Research, The Affiliated Drum Tower Hospital of Nanjing University Medical School, Nanjing, Jiangsu, 210008, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Center for Clinic Stem Cell Research
The Affiliated Drum Tower Hospital of Nanjing University Medical School

ce:affiliation.ce:label: f
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing, 100049, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:label: g
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Xi’an Jiaotong University, Xi’an, Shannxi, 710061, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: h
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: University of Science and Technology of China, Hefei, Anhui, 230026, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Life Science, Shaoxing University, Zhejiang, 312000, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are emerging as an ideal agent for efficient stem cell labeling. In current study, we synthesized a series of CQDs carrying different surface charges by changing the mass ratio of diammonium citrate (DC) and spermidine (Spd), and evaluated the effects of different surface charges on the cytotoxicity, cellular uptake, stability in human umbilical cord derived mesenchymal stem cells (hUCMSCs). We ascertained the optimal labeling time (24 h) and subtoxic concentration (50 μg/mL) of all different charged CQDs. Our results demonstrated that, although positively charged CQDs are more cytotoxic and have lower photoluminescence (PL) compared to negative CQDs, they still have higher labeling efficiency for their higher uptake capacity. We found that relatively weak positive surface charges enabled CQDs to possess good biocompatibility and labeling efficiency in hUCMSCs. This work will helpfully contribute to the design and optimization of CQDs for tracking stem cells and further benefit to clinical research and application.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Human umbilical cord derived mesenchymal stem cells

ce:section.ce:para: Mesenchymal stem cell (MSC), one kind of multipotent stem cell, has generated a great deal of excitement as a potential source of cell-based therapies [1–5]. After transplantation in the host organism, it is needed to monitor the fate of transplanted cells, including their distribution, survival, differentiation, and longevity over time [6–9]. Quantum dots (QDs) were usually composed by groups of II-VI or III-V elements, and their sizes ranged from 2 to 10 nm [10]. Compared with traditional fluorophores such as GFP, FITC and rhodamine, QDs possess many advantages due to their optical properties for cellular label and imaging, such as remarkable resistance photo bleaching, high fluorescence efficiency, size-tunable emission, broader absorption spectra and narrower emission spectra [11]. Compared with luciferase used for cell imaging, QDs avoid cellular aberrance potentially caused by integration of exogenous luciferase gene and the side effect of substrate (luciferin) on cells.

The carbon based QDs (CQDs) were regarded as promising probes for in vitro and in vivo imaging applications owing to their superior optical properties over conventional QDs, such as relatively high chemical and optical stabilities, superior water dispersibility and excellent biocompatibility [12–14]. However, some studies reported that different synthetic methods, particle sizes, and surface chemistry modifications affected the biological toxicities and uptake of CQDs [15–17]. So far, CQDs potential cytotoxicity and instability in biological environment are still ambiguous.

The effects of surface charge on uptake and biological function of different nanoparticles in MSCs have been widely studied. One study reported that positively charged mesoporous silica nanoparticles (MSNs) were quite biocompatible in 3T3-L1 cells and human MSCs (hMSCs); the uptake of MSNs by hMSCs could be regulated by a threshold of positive surface charge and the modulation of surface charge on the uptake was specific to cell type [18]. Chen et al. reported that positively charged hydroxyapatite (HAP) nanoparticles showed stronger improvement for osteoblast cell viability and cell proliferation [19]. Li et al. demonstrated that gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) with different surface charges induced differential cell response on MSCs osteogenesis [20]. In their study, positively charged AuNPs-NH3 showed higher cellular uptake and less effect on alkaline phosphatase (ALP) activity and matrix mineralization in hMSCs compared to negatively charged AuNPs-COOH. Nevertheless, the effects of surface charge on cytotoxicity and uptake of CQDs in hMSCs have not been studied in depth.

In this study, based on one-step carbonization method [21], we developed a novel straightforward method to synthesize CQDs carrying different surface charges by changing the mass ratios of diammonium citrate (DC) and spermidine (Spd). Furthermore, we investigated the effects of different surface charges on CQDs cell-uptake and cytotoxicity, and on proliferation, stemness, self-renewal and differentiation capacity of human umbilical cord derived mesenchymal stem cells (hUCMSCs). Our data suggested that relatively weak positive surface charges enabled CQDs to strike good balance between biocompatibility and labeling efficiency in hUCMSCs. This study provides helpful insight into the design of CQDs for tracking stem cells and further benefit to clinical research and application of CQDs.

CQDs were synthesized using a modified bottom-up carbonization method based on one previously reported [21]. Like Table S1, different mass ratios (1:0, 1:0.25, 1:1, 1:2.5) of diammonium citrate (DC) and spermidine (Spd) were mixed and ground with an agate mortar and pestle for 5 min. Then, the mixtures were moved in beaker and heated at 180 °C for 2 h in a heating furnace. After cooling down to room temperature, the residues were dissolved in 10 mL water and sonicated for 1 h. After centrifugation by 35,000 g for 30 min at 4 °C, the collected supernatants were dialyzed against deionized water through a dialysis membrane (MWCO = 1.0 kD) for 5 h. Subsequently, the purified supernatants were lyophilized and dissolved in water. Thereto, four different CQD samples synthesized with different mass ratios of DC and Spd were obtained and named by C-a, C-b, C-c and C-d. The CQDs were put into 4 °C refrigerator for long-term storage. Here, Spd alone was also considered as one source of carbon and used to synthetize CQD following the same process. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of the CQDs were acquired using Tecnai G2 F20 S-Twin TEM (FEI, USA). The zeta potentials of CQDs in water solution were measured using Zetasize (ZEN3600; Malvern Instruments, UK). Ultraviolet-visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra of CQDs solutions were recorded using monochromatic microplate spectrophotometer (Synergy 4 Multi-Mode; Biotek Instruments, USA). The photoluminescence (PL) spectra of CQDs dispersion were measured by multilabel counter ARVO mx 1420 (ParkinElmer, MA).

The hUCMSCs were gifted and the cells qualification was identified by Nanjing Gulou Hospital. The study was approved by Local Ethics Committee, and all participants were given informed consent to participate. Cells were cultured in basal Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Medium/Nutrient Mixture F-12 (DMEM/F-12) supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS, Gibco) and 1% penicillin-streptomycin (Beyotime Institute of Biotechnology, Jiangsu, China) in a humidified atmosphere of 5% CO2 in air at 37 °C. The hUCMSCs within passage 8 were used in all experiments.

Cell viability was assessed using a cell counting kit-8 (CCK8, Beyotime, China). Briefly, cells were co-cultured with different concentrations of CQDs in 96-well plates for 72 h at a density of 5 × 103 cells/well. CCK8 reagent (10 μL) was then added to each well and incubated for another 4 h before measurement. The absorbance at 450 nm was measured using Multilabel Reader (PerkinElmer, Singapore). The relative cell viability was determined by absorbance values of cells treated with CQDs normalized to those of cells without CQDs treatment control.

Intracellular ROS were detected using the oxidant-sensitive dye carboxy-H2DCFDA (Invitrogen). Cells were plated at a density of 5 × 103 cells/well in 96-well plates and cultured for 24 h, and then co-cultured with CQDs (50 and 100 μg/mL) for the other 24 h. After the incubation, the cells were added with 100
μ L of 100 μM carboxy-H2DCFDA, and cultured for 30 min at 37 °C. The fluorescence intensity in each well was measured at an excitation of 485 nm and an emission of 585 nm. To eliminate the effect of fluorescence of CQDs itself on DCF fluorescence detection, the cells labeled with different types of CQDs and without adding carboxy-H2DCFDA were used as zero setting controls for each type of CQDs treatment.

The hUCMSCs were plated in Confocal culture dish and culture for overnight, and then co-cultured with CQDs (50 μg/mL) for 24 h. After incubation, cells were washed twice with PBS to remove unlabeled CQDs and fixed with fixing solution (Beyotime, China). The fluorescence images of hUCMSCs labeled by CQDs were acquired by Confocal laser scanning microscopy (Nikon A1, Japan). The fluorescent intensity of CQDs in the cells was analyzed using ImageJ software.

To assay uptake rate of CQDs affected by varying incubation times, hUCMSCs were plated at a density of 5 × 104 cells per well in 12-well plates and then co-cultured with 50 μg/mL of CQDs for 0, 3, 6, 9, 12, 24, 48 and 72 h. To assay uptake rate affected by varying CQDs concentrations, hUCMSCs were plated at a density of 5 × 104 cells per well in 12-well plates and then co-cultured with different concentrations of CQDs (0, 50, 100, 200, and 400 μg/mL) for 24 h. After co-cultured with CQDs, cells were harvested and washed twice with PBS, and then analyzed by flow cytometry (BD Accuri C6). The FITC fluorescence channel (FL-1) was used to detect cellular uptake of CQDs. The hUCMSCs without CQDs treatment were used as no detectable control for gating the CQDs labeled cells. Following the method described before [22], labeling efficiency was represented as the percentage of detectable cells by flow cytometry in FITC fluorescence channel from total 5000 tested cells. Labeling efficiencies of different types of CQDs (50 μg/mL) in hUCMSCs were analyzed and compared after 24 h of co-culture. The retention efficiency of each type of CQDs in hUCMSCs was determined by the ratio of labeling efficiency to corresponding photoluminescence (PL) intensity.

To label the hUCMSCs with CQDs, 2 × 105 cells were plated in one well of 6-well plates and co-cultured with CQDs (50 μg/mL) for 24 h. After labeling, cells proliferation and fluorescence intensity of CQDs in cells were measured at different time points from 0 to 12 days. At each time point, cells were washed twice with PBS to remove unlabeled CQDs and collected by trypsinization. The fluorescent intensity of cells labeled with CQDs was measured with flow cytometry. The stability of CQDs in hUCMSCs was defined by the following formula as previously reported [23].
C Q D s
s t a b i l i t y =
F l o u r e s c e n t
i n t e n s i t y
B a s e ( F l o u r e s c e n t
i n t e n s i t y
d i r e c t l y
a f t e r
l a b l i n g )
× 100

ce:section.ce:section-title: qRT-PCR analysis of mRNAs expression levels
ce:section.ce:para: The hUCMSCs were plated at a density of 2 × 105 cells per well in 6-well plates and co-cultured with CQDs (50 μg/mL) for 48 h. Total RNAs were extracted from cells using the RNAeasy Mini Kit (QIAGEN). The reaction of qRT-PCR was performed in Power SYBR Green PCR Master Mix (Life Technologies, USA). The relative expression levels of were expressed in arbitrary units (a.u.) where the Ct values of the genes (octamer-binding transcription factor 4 (Oct-4), Nanog and sex determining region Y-box 2 (SOX2)) was normalized to that of GAPDH. The primers for qRT-PCR were showed in Supplementary Table S2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of CQDs on the osteogenic and adipogenic differentiations of hUCMSCs
ce:section.ce:para: The hUCMSCs were seeded in 12-well plates at a density of 5 × 104 per well. Then, cells were co-cultured with CQDs (50 μg/mL) for 24 h and washed with PBS for two times to remove unlabeled CQDs. To induce the osteogenic differentiation, hUCMSCs were cultured in osteogenic differentiation medium (primary medium plus 10 nM dexamethasone (Sangon biotech, China), 10 mM β-glycerophosphate (Ark Pharm, USA) and 0.05 mM ascorbic acid (Alfa Aesar, England)). After 21 days of culture, hUCMSCs were fixed in 4% paraformaldehyde for 15 min, and then stained with 2% alizarin red S (Sigma, USA) at pH 4.2 for 30 min. To induce the adipogenic differentiation, hUCMSCs were cultured in adipogenic differentiation medium containing 1 μM dexamethasone, 0.5 mM isobutylmethylxanthine (IBMX), 60 μM indometacin (Alfa Aesar, England) and 5 μg/mL insulin (Solarbio, China). After 14 days of culture, hUCMSCs were fixed in 4% paraformaldehyde for 15 min, and then lipid vacuole formation was detected by Oil-Red O (Sangon biotech, China) staining. The amount of alizarin red S and Oil-Red O was quantified by measuring the absorbance from cell lysates at 490 nm. The relative mineralization rate and Lipid vacuole formation compared to control group was expressed as a percentage: ODsample/ODcontrol × 100%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Single cell sphere formation in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: The hUCMSCs were plated in 6-well plates at a density of 2.5 × 105 cells per well and co-cultured with CQDs (50 μg/mL) for 24 h. Then, cells were harvested and plated at a density of 2 × 104 cells per well in 6 cm plates coated with poly 2-hydroxyethyl methacrylate (Sigma-Aldrich, USA). After cultured for 14 days in 4 mL medium containing 0.5% methylcellulose, the live single cell spheres were stained with Calcein AM (BD Biosciences) and photographed by inverted microscope (Nikon, Japan). The diameters of spheres were measured using the ImageJ software and the numbers of spheres with the diameter ≥ 50 μm were counted.

The experiments were conducted independently three times. The data are presented as average ± standard deviation. Statistical analysis was performed using SPSS 19.0. Differences between the data of two groups were evaluated using Student’s t-test, and p < 0.05 was considered statistically significant.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of CQDs carrying different charges
ce:section.ce:para: By changing the mass ratio of DC and Spd, we synthesized a series of CQDs (C-a, C-b, C-c, and C-d) possessing different amounts of surface charges. Because Spd provides the source of ‐NH3 +, when adding different proportions of Spd in a fixed amount of DC, the Zeta potentials of C-a, C-b, C-c, and C-d were varied from -6.78, -2.78, +4.12 to + 23 mV, respectively (Fig. 1 A). The sizes of all types of as-prepared CQDs were uniform and all monodisperse spheres have a diameter of 3∼5 nm (Fig. 1B). The lattice structures of CQDs featured d-spacing of approximately 0.34 nm (insets in Fig. 1B).

Many factors could affect the optical characteristics of CQD, such as sources, temperature, and passivation [24,25]. In our study, DC, one kind of citrate derivate, generally serves as the source of carbon in the top-down method. Spd provides the source of -NH3 +. Zhang et al. reported that the change of “N” contents could achieve carbon dots with tunable luminescence properties [26]. Moreover, Spd also could be considered as one source of carbon, and used to synthetize CQD. As shown in Fig. S1, CQD synthetized by Spd alone showed a weak fluorescence after UV excitation compared to DC. When adding more and more mounts of Spd to a fixed amount of DC, the fluorescence of corresponding CQDs (C-a, C-b, C-c, and C-d) also became to be weaker and weaker (inserts in Fig. 1D)

The data of UV-Vis spectrum showed that the typical absorption peaks of all CQDs aqueous solutions ranged from 335 nm to 365 nm (Fig. 1C), which was due to overlapping n → π∗ transitions in the CO and π→ π∗ in the conjugated CC bonds [27]. Under bright field, unlike colorless CQDs solutions (C-b, C-c, C-d and only Spd), C-a CQDs solution, synthesized only by DC, had a brown color at the same concentration of 0.1 mg/mL (insets in Fig. 1C and Fig. S1). To further explore the optical properties of water soluble CQDs, PL spectra were detected at the excitation wavelength of 365 nm. We found that the PL intensities of the CQDs were C-a > C-b > C-c > C-d and all of them showed the peaks at approximately 450 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity effects of CQDs on hUCMSCs
ce:section.ce:para: We investigated the cytotoxicity effects of different surface charges of CQDs on hUCMSCs after 72 h of CQDs treatment. As shown in Fig. 2 A, all of CQDs exerted no obvious impact on cell viability at the concentration of 50 μg/mL. Unlike C-a, C-b and C-c, C-d significantly (p < 0.05) decreased the cell viability at the concentrations of 100 ∼ 400 μg/mL.

High level of intracellular ROS was generally regarded to cause cell impairment. At the concentration of 50 μg/mL, all four types of CQDs caused no obvious oxidative stress on hUCMSCs (Fig. 2B). At the concentration of 100 μg/mL, only C-d CQDs caused significant oxidative stress on cells (Fig. 2C). Based on these results, 50 μg/mL was regarded as a subcytotoxic concentration for all four types of CQDs and was used for cell labeling in this study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of surface charges on the uptake of CQDs in hUCMSCs
ce:section.ce:para: The uptake of tracer agents by cells is an important factor to affect labeling time and efficiency. The uptake kinetic curves of the four types of CQDs were constructed by relative fluorescence intensities of labeled cells as a function of co-culture time points (Fig. 3 A). At each time point, the uptakes rates of C-c and C-d that owned positive charges were markedly higher than those of C-a and C-b that owned negative charges (Fig. 3A). The uptakes of both C-c and C-d CQDs were increased over time, and similarly reached the peak at 24 h time point and kept at steady plateaus subsequently, when the uptake were saturated. At the plateaus after 24 h time point, the relative fluorescence intensities are higher in C-d CQDs labeled cells than in C-c CQDs labeled cells. For C-a and C-b CQDs, their uptakes rates were slightly increased over time and also reached plateaus at 24 h time point. The relative fluorescence intensities of C-a group were higher than C-b group at all time points. Considering 24 h was the plateaus time for all four types of CQDs for hUCMSCs labeling and no cell proliferation is impaired during 24 h of CQDs exposure (Fig. 3D), we regarded 24 h as optimal labeling time in this study.

From short time kinetic curves of CQDs uptake (Fig. 3B), we found small uptake peaks for C-c and C-d CQDs at 6 h time point, and peaks for C-a and C-b CQDs at 3 h time point. After this stage, all the curves achieved platform at 24 h time point. This indicated that, for all four types of CQDs, the uptake and excretion achieved a short time of balance at early hours of CQDs treatment, but the uptake rates increased again to get final uptake-excretion balance after 24 h. We speculate that, at early hours, the CQDs uptake is absolutely dominant and the excretion may be initiated and momently dominant when the amount of CQDs get to a certain threshold in cells, which leads to the early peaks for CQDs at 3 h or 6 h time point when the uptake and excretion achieve a short time of balance. This may be the result of stress response of cells to CQDs invasion. Compared to positive charged C-c and C-d CQDs, shorter time and lower fluorescence intensity of early peaks of C-a and C-b indicated that negative charge weakened CQDs uptake but promoted their excretion. After this stage, the high concentration of CQDs outside cells renders the uptake still dominant till to the new dynamic balance of uptake and excretion at 24 h time point. We noticed that, at initiating stage from 0 to 6 h, the uptake of C-d CQDs was slightly lower than that of C-c CQDs; but after 6 h, the uptake of C-d CQDs was significantly higher than that of C-c CQDs. This indicated that, compared to C-c CQDs, although C-d CQDs had slightly lower cellular uptake ability, they were more susceptible to be retained in cells. This was confirmed by the highest retention efficiency of C-d CQDs in the four types of CQDs (Fig. 3H).

We further investigated the effect of different concentrations of CQDs on their uptake rate. As a result, the uptakes of all four types of CQDs were increased in a dose-dependent manner. Besides, maximum slopes (linear stages) for C-c and C-d CQDs occurred from 0 to 100 μg/mL (Fig. 3C). This means that the uptakes of C-c and C-d CQDs were proportional to the concentrations lower than 100 μg/mL. As expected, the uptakes of positive-charged CQDs (C-c and C-d) were markedly higher than those of negative-charged CQDs (C-a and C-b) at indicated concentrations (50, 100, 200 and 300 μg/mL).

At the defined optimal labeling time (24 h) and subtoxic concentration (50 μg/mL), the labeling efficiencies of four types of CQDs in hUCMSCs were analyzed by flow cytometry. As Fig. 3E and F showed, the labeling efficiencies of C-a, C-b, C-c and C-d were about 22.4%, 14.2%, 61.4% and 66.1%, respectively. The intracellular distribution of CQDs in hUCMSCs was observed by fluorescent microscope (Fig. 3G). The internalized CQDs were localized in the periphery of nucleus and the retention amounts of C-c and C-d CQDs were intuitively more than those of C-a and C-b CQDs in hUCMSCs. The fluorescent density of C-c and C-d CQDs was about 2 fold more than that of C-a and C-b (Fig. 3I). This result suggested that more C-c and C-d CQDs were entrapped in hUCMSCs compared to C-a and C-b CQDs

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of surface charges on the stability of CQDs in hUCMSCs
ce:section.ce:para: To be used as stem cell trackers, internalized CQDs should be highly stable over an extended period of time. The fluorescence intensities of CQDs in hUCMSCs were quantitatively assessed by flow cytometry. Irrespective of surface charges, the fluorescence intensities and the stability of all four types of CQDs were decreased over time, and disappeared after 12 days of cell expansion (Fig. 4 A and B). But, the stability of C-c and C-d CQDs was significantly higher than that of C-a and C-b CQDs (Fig. 4B).

It has been reported that cell proliferation is a main influencing factor on CQDs stability [28]. The proliferation of hUCMSCs labeled with CQDs increased in a time-dependent manner (Fig. 4C). And, C-a, C-b and C-c CQDs did not cause evidently negative effects on hUCMSCs proliferation at 1, 2, 4 and 7 days of culture. But, C-d CQDs significantly inhibited cell proliferation at 4 and 7 days of culture. These results suggested that relatively high positive surface charge led CQDs to cause negative effects on hUCMSCs proliferation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of CQDs on stem cell potentials of hUCMSCs
ce:section.ce:para: Stem cells exhibit two cardinal abilities to self-renewal and differentiate. Single cell sphere formation has widely used to identify self-renewal capacity of stem cells [29]. Our results demonstrated that all four types of CQDs-labeled hUCMSCs were able to generate single cell sphere after 14 days of suspension culture (Fig. 5 A), and the CQDs exerted no obviously adverse effect on the ability of single cell sphere formation, compared to control group (Fig. 5C).

MSCs have widely used in tissue regeneration research field for versatile differentiation capacity to various lineages including osteoblasts, adipocytes and neurons [30,31]. CQDs have been reported to promote osteogenic differentiation of MSCs [32]. In this study, osteogenic and adipogenic differentiations were induced to evaluate the effect of the four groups of CQDs on the differentiation capacity of hUCMSCs. We found that only the highest positively charged C-d significantly reduced the osteogenic and adipogenic differentiation abilities of hUCMSCs, but other CQDs exerted no adverse effect on differentiation of hUCMSCs (Fig. 5B, D and E).

To investigate the effects of CQDs on the stemness of hUCMSCs, the transcription of Oct-4, Nanog and SOX2, key transcription factors highly associated with the stemness of stem cells, were measured by qRT-PCR method. We found that all four types of CQDs exerted no adverse effect on the mRNAs expressions of Oct-4, Nanog and SOX2 (Fig. 5F, G and H).

For the excellent PL properties and good biocompatibility, CQDs were regarded as potential tracers for cell tracking [12,33–36]. Many factors may affect the cytotoxicity and cellular labeling efficiency of CQDs, such as size and shape. The surface charge is certainly one of the most important impacting parameter. But so far, the effects of surface charge on uptake and cytotoxicity of CQDs in hUCMSCs have not been reported. Here, for the first time, we synthesized the CQDs carrying different surface charges with an optimized one-step method and evaluated the effects of different surface charges on uptake of CQDs in hUCMSCs, and on hUCMSCs properties after labeling.

Generally, top-down and bottom-up methods are used for CQDs synthesis [30]. Top-down methods include arc-discharge and laser ablation of carbon targets [15–17]; bottom-up methods include oxidation, microwave-assisted pyrolysis and hydrothermal carbonization [15–17]. In most studies, generally, CQDs were initially synthesized as a core that is similar to free carbon nanoparticles without significant toxic effects [37]; and then, the passivation and modification were further performed to endow CQDs various functions. However, some agents used for additional passivation and modification may result in extra cytotoxicity of CQDs. It was reported that the cytotoxicity of CQDs was mainly dependent on the chemical nature of the surface passivation agent [38]. Similarly, Yang et al. reported that the PEG used for CQDs surface passivation caused cytotoxicity [37]. Based on these concerns, Pi et al. developed a simple one-step dry heating method to synthesize mannose-modified fluorescent CQDs from solid ammonium citrate and mannose to avoid additional passivation and modification, and then avoid more potential cytotoxic effects [39]. Here, we optimized this method and successfully synthesized a series of CQDs possessing different amounts of surface charges by changing the mass ratios of DC and Spd.

Potential cytotoxicity is the key issue in evaluating the utility of CQDs as cell trackers [30]. Factors such as dose and exposure time are mainly involved in CQD cytotoxicity [40]. In this study, the subtoxic concentration of CQDs in the study was first determined for the application in the rest of this research. This subtoxic concentration was consistent with other studies in which concentration lower than 50 μg/mL caused no cytotoxicity of citric acid-based CQDs to rat MSCs [32], and 50 μg/mL was regarded as the threshold dose for toxicity of graphene quantum dots to hMSCs [41]. Although some studies reported that positively charged nanoparticles were quite biocompatible and even improve the cell viability and proliferation [18,19], some studies reported the opposite results [17,42,43]. In this study, C-d CQDs had significant cytotoxicity to hUCMSCs, which suggested that higher positive surface charges are more cytotoxic. This was consistent with the other report that positively charged CQDs were the most cytotoxic to NIH/3T3 cells compared to other neutral and negatively charged CQDs [17,42].

Generally, cellular uptake of nanoparticles was highly dependent upon the sizes and charges of nanoparticles. In our study, the sizes of all four types of CQDs we synthesized are uniform with even diameter of 3∼5 nm. Therefore, the surface charge was considered as the main factor to affect cellular uptake for all four types of CQDs. Our results agreed with the most previous studies in which the increase of positive or negative surface charge led to increased CQDs uptake in comparison with less charged CQDs [44]. In some studies, the time reaching saturation of the intracellular nanoparticle concentration was reported from several hours to several days [45,46]. Here, the 24 h of saturation time of CQDs almost match the timescale of the hUCMSCs population doubling time (～25 h). J.A. Kim et al. reported the same observation in their nanoparticle uptake research. They reported that the different cell cycle phase affected cellular uptake of nanoparticle [47]. Maybe only after most of cells went through a complete cell cycle under CQDs exposure could the uptake curves reach the plateau. The uptakes of CQDs were increased when the cells were exposed to higher CQDs concentrations, which suggested that the so-called saturation was the dynamic balance between the intracellular CQDs load and extracellular CQDs concentration. Taken together, the uptake kinetic behavior of each type of CQDs could result from cell division, CQDs concentration, and dynamic balance between uptake and excretion of CQDs. In the context of same proliferation of labeled cells, and the same shape, size and concentration of all four types of CQDs, the different surface charges might predominantly affect the uptake kinetic behaviors. Our results also indicated that positive surface charge improved CQDs uptake and retention, and then improved labeling efficiency in hUCMSCs.

CQDs maintenance within the labeled cells is an important criterion for reliable cell tracking over an extended period of time. Studies reported that the stability of various QDs ranged from a few days to several weeks, which was associated with dose dilution caused by cell division, QD excretion, fluorescence signal reduction and innate instability of nano-particles [23]. We found that positive surface charges, even low level positive charges (C-c, + 4.12 mV), made the CQDs more stable in hUCMSCs than negative charges did. Although it was reported that gadolinium (III) functionalized fluorescent carbon dots promoted the proliferation of hMSCs [48], our results demonstrated that CQDs did not cause favorable effect on proliferation and relatively high positive surface charge caused negative effect on hUCMSCs proliferation.

As one kind of multipotent stem cell, the hUCMSCs have the capacity of self-renewal and differentiation. In our study, only C-d CQDs carrying the highest positive surface charge disrupted the osteogenic and adipogenic differentiation potential of hUCMSCs, which was consistent with the finding that C-d CQDs had relatively poor biocompatibility compared to C-a, C-b and C-c CQDs. Although positive charge endows CQDs some merits contributing to cellular labeling efficiency, it was not the stronger the better. Apparently, CQD carrying relatively low positive surface charges, such as C-c ( + 4.12 mV), possesses good biocompatibility and labeling efficiency. Of course, further in-depth detailed studies are needed to clarify the optimum surface charge perfectly balancing biocompatibility and labeling efficiency of CQDs for tracking hUCMSCs. Overall, our research will benefit to improvements in the design of CQDs for various biomedical applications with stem cells.

CQDs carrying different surface charges were fabricated by a new and straightforward method. Here, we characterized the properties of these CQDs and preliminarily investigated the effects of different surface charges of CQDs on the hUCMSCs viability, proliferation, differentiation, and on the cellular uptake and stability of CQDs. Higher positively charged CQDs possess lower level of PL and relatively poor biocompatibility, but higher level of cellular uptake and stability afford them good cellular labeling efficiency. Lower positively charged CQDs are optimal option for hUCMSCs labeling for their good balancing between biocompatibility and cellular uptake.
The authors have no conflicts of interest to declare.

This work has been generously supported by Ministry of Science and Technology (MOST) (Grant 2017YFA0104301), National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant 31471307), (MOST) (Grant 2014CB965003), and One Hundred Person Project from Chinese Academy of Sciences to G.S, as well the scientific research start-up funding of Shaoxing University (Grant No. 20185009).

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors would like to thank Chih-Ching Huang and Chia-Hua Lin for provide material of CQDs. The authors would also like to thank Guangli Suo for the study design, guidance and paper writing.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.colsurfb.2018.07.034.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:
